text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy for a posterior cervical fibroid, beginning by opening the retroperitoneum in the triangle between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament. The uterine artery is identified crossing from lateral to medial, with the ureter visible approximately one centimeter caudal and medial to it. Vascular clips are applied to the uterine artery, spaced about 0.5 centimeters apart, and the same procedure is repeated on the opposite side.

The surgeon clips both the uterine and vaginal arteries to minimize bleeding. An incision is made over the most prominent part of the fibroid on the posterior cervix, followed by enucleation of the fibroid from the myoma bed. The clipped arteries help reduce bleeding during the dissection of the fibroid from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon lateralizes the right ureter to the level of the ureteric tunnel to facilitate suturing at the posterior cervix. Using a quill suture, the surgeon performs suturing in two layers to obliterate the myoma bed, aided by a three-dimensional laparoscope for enhanced visualization. After suturing, the remaining myoma is enucleated from the peritoneal cavity using a disposable morcellator blade.
